event: Abraham visited by three angels at Mamre
symbols: three angelic visitors — the mysterious theophany at Mamre, understood by the Church Fathers as a prefiguration of the Trinity
aged Abraham kneeling — the faithful patriarch's humility and hospitality before the divine
clouds surrounding the angels — the heavenly realm breaking into the earthly, the glory of God's presence
youthful beauty of the angels — the ageless and incorruptible nature of divine messengers
description: An elderly bearded man in dark robes kneels or sits at the lower left, looking upward in awe. Before him stands a youthful, luminous angelic figure draped in a golden-yellow cloth, with large wings visible. Behind and beside this angel, two more figures are partially visible amid swirling clouds. The angel's body is idealized and radiant. The composition is set against a dramatic cloudy sky, with warm golden light illuminating the central angel while darker tones surround the aged patriarch.
theological_description: The visit of the three angels to Abraham at the oaks of Mamre (Genesis 18:1-15) is one of the most theologically rich episodes in the Old Testament. The text states that 'the LORD appeared to him,' yet Abraham sees three men, leading the Church Fathers — particularly Augustine and Ambrose — to read this as a prefiguration of the Trinity: three persons, one God. Abraham's hospitality becomes paradigmatic; the Epistle to the Hebrews urges believers to show hospitality to strangers, 'for thereby some have entertained angels unawares' (Heb 13:2). The angelic visitors announce the birth of Isaac to the aged Sarah, a promise that defied natural possibility and demonstrated that 'nothing is too hard for the LORD' (Gen 18:14). This miraculous birth prefigures the greater miracle of the Incarnation, where the Virgin Mary conceives by the Holy Spirit. Abraham's faithful reception of the divine promise, despite its apparent impossibility, is the model of faith that Paul extols in Romans 4: he 'believed God, and it was counted to him as righteousness.'

scripture_refs.reason: The account of the three visitors appearing to Abraham at Mamre and announcing Isaac's birth
The exhortation to hospitality, for some have entertained angels unawares — directly referencing this episode
The Lord's declaration that nothing is too hard for Him, the heart of the promise to Abraham
Paul's exposition of Abraham's faith in believing the impossible promise of a son
Gabriel's words to Mary echo Gen 18:14 — nothing is impossible with God — linking Mamre to the Annunciation
